text.title: Fitness Function Wiring Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules turn each (High-impact, High-likelihood) QAA scenario into a concrete CI job. Every scenario gets a tool, a config artefact, a threshold, a cadence, and a breach action — no prose-only obligations survive.

rule.statement: Every (H,H) scenario in the QAA register has exactly one canonical CI job named fitness-<scenario-id>, owned by a single team, with the tool fixed by quality attribute: k6 or Gatling for throughput/latency, Chaos Monkey / Litmus / Toxiproxy for resilience, OWASP ZAP for runtime AppSec, Trivy / Grype for supply-chain. No (H,H) scenario is allowed without a named job.
rule.rationale: When scenarios point to "we will measure this" without a named CI job, the obligation evaporates between architecture review and engineering. One job, one owner, one tool removes the diffusion.

rule.statement: Each fitness job's threshold is expressed in the numeric units of the agreed cost + latency budget (e.g. p99 ≤ 250ms, error_rate ≤ 0.1%, MTTR ≤ 5min, container-CVE count = 0 critical) and is stored as code next to the job (`fitness/<scenario-id>.yaml`), not hidden in a dashboard.
rule.rationale: A threshold stored only in a Grafana panel cannot block a deploy. Co-locating the threshold with the job makes it reviewable in PRs and prevents silent drift.

rule.statement: Each job has a declared cadence: per-PR (lightweight smoke), nightly (full load profile), weekly (chaos / ZAP), or release-gate (must pass before tag). The cadence is part of the architecture-as-code repo and is reviewed in the monthly architecture review.
rule.rationale: Without cadence-as-code, fitness jobs decay into "we used to run this". Pinning cadence into the same review cycle as the architecture keeps verification frequency proportional to architectural risk.

rule.statement: Every fitness job declares a breach-action of one of: block-merge, block-release, page-on-call, open-ticket. The action is mechanical (CI exits non-zero, PagerDuty triggers, Jira issue auto-created) — never "notify the architect by email".

rationale.source: Bridges architecture and CI; breach actions must be enforceable

rule.rationale: Email-only breach actions get filtered or ignored; mechanical actions force the conversation. Choosing the correct action by scenario keeps the noise budget aligned with the risk score.

rule.statement: The QAA register and the fitness/ directory are link-checked in CI: any (H,H) scenario without a corresponding `fitness/<id>.yaml` and any orphan fitness file without a register entry fails the architecture-as-code build. The monthly review explicitly reads the link-check output before approving the next iteration.
rule.rationale: A two-way link check is the only mechanism that prevents the register and the CI surface from quietly drifting apart over a quarter. Without it, the methodology degrades to good intentions.

rule.statement: If preconditions in AGENTS.md 'Applies If' do not all hold, skip this methodology and route to the correct one; do not retro-fit inputs.
rule.rationale: Forcing a methodology onto a misaligned task produces artefacts downstream consumers cannot trust.

example.title: Wiring a checkout-latency (H,H) scenario
example.code:
QAA register entry: id: SC-014 attribute: performance scenario: "checkout submit p99 ≤ 250ms under 200 RPS, error_rate ≤ 0.1%" impact: H likelihood: H
fitness/SC-014.yaml: job_name: fitness-SC-014 tool: k6 config: fitness/SC-014.k6.js # 200 RPS, 10 min, ramp 1 min thresholds: http_req_duration_p99_ms: { lte: 250 } http_req_failed_rate: { lte: 0.001 } cadence: nightly + release-gate breach_action: block-release owner: payments-team
CI: - PR check: link-check passes (SC-014 ↔ fitness/SC-014.yaml) - Nightly: k6 run; on threshold breach → exit 1, GitHub issue auto-opened - Release tag: k6 must pass; otherwise tag job halts
Monthly architecture review: - Read link-check output: 0 orphans - Confirm SC-014 thresholds still match budget; rotate config if not
example.note: The architecture decision ("checkout must be fast under load") is now a mechanically enforced contract. Drift triggers a release-block, not a quarterly surprise.
